blocks.text: Dear Ms. Sutton:

This letter confirms that {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} has retained Ashcombe Advisory Partners to lead its communications through the matter now in front of it, and it sets out what we will do, who will do it, what it costs, and the terms we both agree to. Crisis work turns on being clear before you are asked to be, and this letter is the first place we get to be clear with each other. If anything below does not match the conversation we had, tell me before you sign rather than after.
Scope of the Engagement

The engagement is a single body of work: to help {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} say the right thing, to the right people, in the right order, while a difficult situation is live and afterward as it settles. That means a position we can defend, a small set of messages agreed in writing, and the discipline to hold to them when the pressure is to improvise. We are not here to decide the underlying matter or to speak for you. We are here to make sure that what you choose to say is said the same way twice and lands where it should, and that the people who speak for the company are never left guessing at the current line.

Our approach in this kind of work is plain, and we have used it for years. We start by getting the facts straight and the audiences named, because a message built on a shaky fact does more harm than silence. From there we agree the core lines and a holding position in writing, so that no one on your side is caught inventing an answer in the moment. We prepare for the questions we can see coming and the two or three we hope never arrive. Through the live period we stay close and reachable, and we would rather you call us with a small thing early than sit on a large one until it has grown.
The Team

Amy Carter will carry this engagement day to day and is your point of contact for anything of substance. She checks every claim against its source before it goes near a draft, which is exactly the habit this work rewards, and she is the person who will turn a worry you raise in the morning into a concrete step by the afternoon. When you need something moved, go to Amy Carter first.

Nancy Suarez will draft and shape the messaging itself. She has a good ear for the line that actually lands rather than the one that merely covers the point, and she will tell you plainly when a wording is doing less than you think it is. Between Nancy Suarez and Amy Carter you will always know what has changed and what is still open.

I lead this firm and I read the work that leaves it. I will not be on every call week to week, but I am reachable directly, and in a matter like this one I expect to be. If the work is not going the way you need it to, I would far rather hear it from you first than read about it later.
Fees and Payment

Our fee for this engagement is {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}}, fixed at the outset and quoted as a single figure rather than a rate against hours. We work to a fixed fee because it keeps our attention on the outcome instead of the clock, and because you should be able to call us with a question without watching a meter run. The fee is billed in equal installments across the term, the first on commencement. Reasonable out-of-pocket costs that you approve in advance, such as travel to meet your stakeholders or the production of finished materials, are billed at cost and itemized on each invoice. Invoices are payable within thirty days of receipt.
Term and Termination

The engagement commences on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}} and runs approximately eight months, which is the basis on which we have planned the work, though crisis timelines rarely keep to the calendar and we will flag it early if the matter demands more or less. Either of us may end the engagement on fifteen days' written notice. If you end it, you owe the installments that have fallen due as of that date and the approved costs incurred to it, and nothing further. If we end it, which we would do only if the work as agreed had become work we could not do honestly, we will return any fee already paid for work not yet performed.
Confidentiality

Discretion is the whole of this work. We will treat your internal information, your plans, and the fact and content of this engagement as confidential, and we will use anything you tell us about {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} that is not already public only to do the work you have retained us for, sharing it no further than the people on each side who need it. We ask the same of you regarding our methods and our materials. Where a statement or a disclosure carries its own timing, we follow yours and not our own convenience.
Limitation of Liability

We will carry out this engagement with the care and skill customary in our profession. We do not control how a reporter frames a story, how an audience receives a message, or how a situation moves on a given day, and we do not warrant a particular result, because the parts that decide it are not ours to command. Our total liability under this engagement, on any theory, is limited to the fees actually paid to us for it, and neither of us is liable to the other for indirect or consequential loss. Nothing in this paragraph limits liability for fraud or willful misconduct, and nothing should.
Governing Law

This letter is the entire agreement between us on this engagement and replaces anything said or drafted before it. It is governed by the law of the state in which this firm keeps its principal office, and any dispute we cannot settle between ourselves goes to the courts sitting in that state, to whose jurisdiction we each consent. Amendments take effect only in writing signed by both of us.
Acceptance

If this letter reflects our agreement, please sign below and return one copy to me. Amy Carter will be in touch within the week to set the kickoff and to agree the first pieces of work. We do not take a matter like this lightly, and I am glad {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} has asked us to stand with you through it.
Very truly yours,

Our engagement with {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} commenced on {{fact:f:E-2021-001.start}}, and this memo sets out how the team will run the opening weeks now that the letter is signed. The objective for this phase is narrow and worth stating plainly: get the facts of the matter documented and verified, name every audience that will need to hear from the company, and put a holding position in place before anyone is forced to improvise one. Nothing external moves until those three are settled. I would rather we spend the first weeks being sure of the ground than move quickly on a message we later have to walk back.
I have divided the early work into the workstreams below, with an owner named for each so nothing sits in the gap between us:

items: Fact base. Amy Carter to assemble and verify the timeline and the underlying facts, checking each against its source before it enters any draft. Open questions go to Megan Sutton as they arise, not in a batch at the end.
Audience map. Nancy Suarez and Amy Carter to list every internal and external audience, note what each already believes, and mark the ones we may need to correct.
Core messages and holding lines. Nancy Suarez to draft the position and the holding statement, kept short and agreed in writing before either travels.
Anticipated questions. The team to prepare an agreed answer for the questions we can see coming and a holding response for the two or three we hope never arrive.
Client coordination. Megan Sutton to be our single point of contact at {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} for approvals and for anything that touches operations.

blocks.text: A few next steps. I will circulate a short intake questionnaire to Megan Sutton this week so both sides are working from the same facts, and I will flag any point where the documents and the account we were given do not line up. Nancy Suarez will bring a first cut of the holding lines to our internal review before anything goes to the client. We will keep a single status thread running so decisions are confirmed in writing and no one is guessing at the current version. If I have misjudged a priority here, or if there is a workstream I have missed, tell me early; this is the phase where a correction is cheap and a wrong assumption is not.
Prepared by Amy Carter.
Megan Sutton,

Opening a running thread for the {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} work so we have one place to track it and the record stays in order. We are a few weeks in and about where I expected: the fact base is assembled and I am most of the way through verifying it against the source documents your team sent over, the audience map is drafted, and Nancy Suarez has a first cut of the holding lines in hand.

Two things would help before we go further. First, could you confirm who on your side has final sign-off on external language, so we route approvals to the right person the first time? Second, there is one gap in the timeline I could not close from the documents alone; I will send the specific question separately today, and a quick answer will let us lock the holding statement.

Once the holding lines are settled, Nancy Suarez and I would like thirty minutes with you to walk the audience map before anything is circulated. Send a couple of windows that work on your side this week and I will set it up.

Best,
Amy Carter
Meeting Minutes: Crisis Communications for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}
Attendees
Discussion

The headline from this session: the position holds, and the work from here is discipline, not invention. We are far enough into the engagement that the messages are settled and have been tested in use; the risk now is drift, not a blank page, and the meeting was about keeping the settled work settled.

Nancy Suarez opened by walking the group through the core position and the holding lines as they stand, noting which have already been used and how they landed. Amy Carter reported that the fact base is fully verified and that the two questions outstanding at the last check have been closed against source. Megan Sutton confirmed that leadership at {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} is comfortable with the current framing and that nothing in the underlying matter has shifted the ground beneath it.

The bulk of the discussion turned on sequencing. With the language settled, the temptation on the client side is to begin using it more freely than the plan allows, and Amy Carter flagged that as the single risk most worth watching between now and the next review. Megan Sutton agreed to remind the internal owners that no external statement moves ahead of the agreed sequence. Nancy Suarez took the point that two of the holding lines have felt long in practice and should be tightened before they are used again.

On cadence, the group agreed to keep the weekly status thread running and to confirm any interim decision in writing so the operations team is never guessing at the current version. Megan Sutton asked for a short one-page summary of the live messages she can keep beside her; Amy Carter will produce it. The group agreed to reconvene for a full working session once the tightened lines and the summary have been circulated.
Action Items

rows: Tighten the two holding lines flagged as overlong
Produce a one-page summary of the live messages
Remind internal owners to hold to the agreed sequence
Reconfirm the verified fact base against any new development

blocks.text: Minutes prepared by Nancy Suarez.
Status Report: Crisis Communications for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}
Summary

At the midpoint of the engagement, the {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} communications work is on plan. The position is settled, the holding lines have been tested in practice, and the focus has shifted from building the messages to holding to them and preparing for the questions still ahead. Nothing in the period changed the direction of the work, and the engagement is tracking within the budget of {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}} agreed at the outset.
Accomplishments This Period

Since the last report the team completed and verified the full fact base and closed the two questions that had been open against source, so that every message now rests on something we can point to. Nancy Suarez finalized the core position and tightened the holding lines that had run long in early use, and the shorter versions have held up well where they have been needed. We produced a one-page summary of the live messages for day-to-day reference on your side, and we walked the audience map with your operations team so that everyone is working from the same list of who hears what, and in what order.

We also put the anticipated-questions material into usable shape. For each audience we now have an agreed answer to the questions we can see coming and a holding response for the two or three we hope never arrive. That is the quiet part of a crisis engagement, and in my experience it is the part that earns its keep, because it turns a hard question into a decision already made rather than one made under pressure.
Upcoming Work

The second half of the engagement is about sequencing and readiness rather than new material. Over the coming weeks we will finalize the order in which the messages are used across audiences, rehearse the responses with the small group who may need to deliver them, and keep the fact base current against any new development. Nancy Suarez will maintain the master copy of the messages and date each change so there is never a question about the current version, and I will keep the weekly status thread running as the single record of decisions. We will also give the external references a consistency pass so the record reads the same way throughout.
Risks

Two risks are worth naming plainly. The first is drift: with the language settled, the natural temptation is to begin using it more freely than the plan allows, and internal discipline on sequence matters more now than it did at the start. The second is timing: a crisis rarely keeps to a calendar, and a development on the underlying matter could compress the schedule quickly. Neither is a cause for concern today, but both are the reason we hold the fact base current and the responses rehearsed rather than waiting to be asked. The work remains within the {{fact:f:E-2021-001.fee}} budget, and I do not foresee that changing on the current scope.
I am glad to walk any of this through in more detail at your convenience. Prepared by Amy Carter.
Meeting Minutes: Crisis Communications for {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}}
Attendees
Discussion

The headline: the acute phase is behind us, and this session was about landing the work well rather than fighting a fire. Most of what we built has now been used, it held, and the conversation has turned to what {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} keeps once we step back.

Nancy Suarez reviewed how the holding lines and the core position performed through the period since the last session, noting that the shorter lines carried the load exactly as we hoped they would. Amy Carter walked the group through the fact base one last time to confirm nothing had drifted, and reported that the anticipated-questions material had covered the questions that actually came. Megan Sutton said leadership at {{fact:f:E-2021-001.client}} was satisfied with how the messaging had carried the matter, and asked what the company should institutionalize before the engagement closes.

That question shaped the rest of the discussion. The group agreed the client should keep three things: the verified fact base, the message summary in its current one-page form, and a short protocol for who owns external language going forward. Nancy Suarez argued for keeping the protocol deliberately short, on the view that a page people actually read beats a binder they file, and the group agreed. Amy Carter noted a small number of external references that will need a final consistency pass so the record reads the same way throughout.

On next steps, the group agreed to assemble a brief handover pack rather than let the materials scatter, and to hold one more working session to walk it through before the work concludes. Megan Sutton will confirm which internal owner inherits the message summary so it does not fall to no one.
Action Items

rows: Draft the short go-forward protocol for external language
Final consistency pass on external references
Assemble the handover pack of live materials
Name the internal owner to inherit the message summary

blocks.text: Minutes prepared by Nancy Suarez.
